prompt.content:
Use file “Cards_Patient 10” to access the entire medical history / record for the patient. Please use the patient tab as the source of truth for all demographic information. Disregard patient identifiers (Name, Age, DOB, MRN) found within the text of the clinicalnotes tab as they have been shifted for de-identification purposes

Patient 10 presents to the clinic for her initial visit at this clinic.

Vitals:  
BP 138/90 mmHg, manually taken
Pulse 82 bpm
Temp 36.9 C (98.5 F) Oral   
Resp 18  bpm
SpO2 98% on RA  
Ht 5 ft   
Wt 123 lb

By comparing the vital signs from this visit to the most recent visit, what is the single most likely diagnosis that should be ruled out? What is the single most important lab test to rule out this diagnosis?

Answers should align with the most current clinical guidelines as of January 10, 2026.

rubrics.name: States that the patient has a history of hypertension. (ver_f5c405512d504672be3b77f0adf5441b)

messages.content: States that the patient has resistant hypertension.

messages.content: States that the patient's lab results show a normal potassium level.

messages.content: Utilizes the 2025 American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association joint committee clinical practice guidelines on hypertension.

messages.content: States that screening for primary aldosteronism in adults with resistant hypertension.

messages.content: States that screening for primary aldosteronism is recommended regardless of potassium status.

rubrics.name: States that primary aldosteronism is the single most likely diagnosis to be... (ver_616f5d66810642e188f71fb31eaf5163)

messages.content: States that primary aldosteronism is the single most likely diagnosis to be ruled out.

rubrics.name: States that the single most important laboratory test to rule out primary... (ver_de9f7759682342daa43b2494b7764b4d)

messages.content: States that the single most important laboratory test to rule out primary aldosteronism is a plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio.

golden_responses.golden_text_response: The patient has a history of hypertension, and their blood pressure was found to be elevated at today's clinic visit compared to the most recent one. They are currently taking three antihypertensive medications and have resistant hypertension. Laboratory results show a normal potassium level. The assessment utilizes the 2025 American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association joint committee clinical practice guidelines on hypertension. It is recommended to screen for primary aldosteronism in adults with resistant hypertension, regardless of potassium status, as primary aldosteronism is the most likely diagnosis to be ruled out. The most important laboratory test for this purpose is the plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio.
